title: Karemi Bhante (the Sāmāyika Vow)
summary: Karemi Bhante is the short vow with which a practitioner formally takes up sāmāyika — a fixed period (traditionally 48 minutes) of equanimity, set apart from worldly activity. Standing before the guru, the reciter declares: 'I undertake equanimity, O reverend one; I renounce all blameworthy activity.' The vow then spells out exactly how completely that renunciation is meant — not doing and not causing such activity, through mind, speech, and body — and closes by confessing and casting off the faulty self of the past. It is recited every time a sāmāyika or a pratikramaṇa is begun, because pratikramaṇa itself is performed within the state of sāmāyika.
ritualPlacement: This is the anchor sūtra of the **first Āvaśyaka, Sāmāiya (Sāmāyika — the equanimity vow)**, and so it opens the practice on which all six obligations rest. In the base Daivasika (Devasi) sequence it is recited after the preparatory units — Iriyāvahiyaṁ with its kāyotsarga, Logassa, Gamaṇāgamaṇo, Jīvarāśi, the Aṭhāra Pāpasthānaka, and the Dravya-Kṣetra-Kāla-Bhāva scope declaration — at which point the reciter actually 'takes' the sāmāyika; the parallel concluding vow is Sāmāyika Pārvānī (the sixth Āvaśyaka, Paccakkhāṇa). Because every pratikramaṇa is performed inside sāmāyika, the same Karemi Bhante is **shared** and recited across Devasi, Rāi, Pakkhī, Cāumāsī, Saṁvatsarī, and the Deśāvagāśika observance; per the book's sharing model, Cāumāsī and Saṁvatsarī carry no independent text but follow the Pākṣika with the word-substitution (પક્ખી → ચઉમાસી / સંવત્સરિ) and the kāyotsarga override. A near-identical adaptation, `deshavagasik-samayik-pachchakkhan`, recasts this same formula for the tenth lay vow.
authorAndSource: Not the composition of a named author. Karemi Bhante is canonical liturgical text — the Sāmāyika-daṇḍaka of the Āvaśyaka-sūtra (the first, Sāmāyika, Āvaśyaka), transmitted in Ardhamāgadhī Prakrit and shared in essentials across the Śvetāmbara traditions. In this book it appears in the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipakṣa) Devasi sequence; the wording, mātrās, and conjuncts are to be honoured exactly as printed there.
structureArc: A single prose vow that moves through four clear movements. (1) **Resolution** — 'Karemi bhante sāmāiyaṁ': I undertake equanimity, O reverend one. (2) **Renunciation** — 'sāvajjaṁ jogaṁ paccakkhāmi, jāva niyamaṁ pajjuvāsāmi': I renounce blameworthy activity, and for as long as I am bound by this rule I will attend upon (this state). (3) **The scope of the renunciation** — 'duvihaṁ tivihaṇaṁ, maṇeṇaṁ vāyāe kāeṇaṁ, na karemi na kāravemi': by two ways and three means — in mind, speech, and body I neither do nor cause to be done (this clause is what makes the vow exhaustive). (4) **Turning back from the past** — 'tassa bhante paḍikkamāmi, nindāmi, garihāmi, appāṇaṁ vosirāmi': from that (past blameworthy activity), O reverend one, I retreat (pratikramaṇa), I censure it before myself, I condemn it before the guru, and I cast off my (faulty) self.
keyThemes: equanimity (sāmāyika) as a bounded vow of time
the exhaustive 'two ways × three means' formula of restraint
the threefold instrument of action: mind, speech, body
the fourfold turning-back: pratikramaṇa, nindā, garhā, vyutsarga
sāmāyika as the ground on which all six Āvaśyakas rest

glossary.definition: The state and vow of equanimity — a set period (traditionally one muhūrta, ~48 minutes) of withdrawing from worldly activity to rest in evenness of mind; the first of the six Āvaśyakas.
'O reverend one' — a respectful vocative addressing the guru (or, in the guru's absence, the established sthāpanā), before whom the vow is declared.
'Blameworthy activity' — any action (yoga = activity of mind, speech, or body) that involves sin or harm; precisely what the vow renounces.
Renunciation / a binding vow of abstention; here, 'paccakkhāmi' = 'I renounce.' Pratyākhyāna is also the sixth Āvaśyaka.
'For as long as (I am bound by) the rule' — the clause fixing the duration of the vow to the period of the sāmāyika.
Devoted attendance / waiting upon; 'pajjuvāsāmi' = 'I attend upon (this state),' i.e. I remain steadfast in equanimity for the vow's duration.
'By two ways and by three means' — the formula defining the reach of the renunciation: two ways = doing (karaṇa) and causing to be done (kārāpaṇa); three means = mind, speech, body.
Doing oneself and causing another to do; 'na karemi, na kāravemi' = 'I neither do nor cause to be done.' (A third mode, approving — anumodanā — is not undertaken in this two-way form.)
The three instruments of all action — mind, speech, and body — through which the renunciation operates.
'Turning back' / retreat from transgression; 'paḍikkamāmi' = 'I withdraw (from that past fault),' the root act that names the whole ritual.
Self-censure — inwardly condemning one's own past fault; 'nindāmi' = 'I censure (it within myself).'
Censure before the guru — openly condemning the fault in the presence of one's teacher; 'garihāmi' = 'I condemn (it before the guru).'
Casting off / abandonment; 'appāṇaṁ vosirāmi' = 'I cast off my (faulty) self,' renouncing the self conditioned by past blameworthy activity.

howToRead: A newcomer can read Karemi Bhante simply as a four-step promise — I take up calm, I give up harmful action, I mean it completely (in deed, word, and thought, neither doing nor causing), and I turn away from my past faults. An advanced practitioner will weigh the precise legal force of 'duvihaṁ tivihaṇaṁ' (two ways × three means, here without the approval-mode) and recognise the closing 'paḍikkamāmi–nindāmi–garihāmi–vosirāmi' as the seed-formula of pratikramaṇa itself, around which every per-verse and per-vidhi reading of this corpus turns.

sources.title: Āvaśyaka-sūtra (Sāmāyika Āvaśyaka, Sāmāyika-daṇḍaka)
sources.consultedFor: Canonical identity of Karemi Bhante as the first-Āvaśyaka Sāmāyika vow and its standard wording.

sources.title: પંચ પ્રતિક્રમણ સૂત્ર – વિધિ સહિત (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipakṣa edition)
sources.consultedFor: Ritual placement in the Devasi sequence, the shared-sūtra substitution rule, and the verbatim Gujarati-script reading.

notes: Word division and orthography follow the book exactly (e.g. 'સાવજજં', 'દુવિહેણં'/'દુવિહં તિવિહેણં', 'પચ્ચખ્ખામિ', 'નિંદામિ', 'ગરિહામિ'); these are not normalised. The phrase 'jāva niyamaṁ' is read here as 'for as long as the rule binds (me),' i.e. fixing the vow's duration; some recensions read 'jāva sāhū' or otherwise, and traditions vary — flagged as a known textual variant rather than treated as settled. The two-way form ('na karemi, na kāravemi') deliberately omits the approving mode (anumodanā) appropriate to the lay sāmāyika vow; this is a doctrinal point of the vow's scope, not an omission in the text. The standard ~48-minute (one muhūrta) duration of sāmāyika is traditional and not asserted by the verse itself.
